messages.content: My dryer stopped heating up, what should I check?

For a house resident: If your dryer has stopped heating up, don't worry, it's usually an easy fix. First, let's check the most common causes. According to your manual, which you can find on page 5, the top three things to check are a blown thermal fuse, a clogged exhaust vent, and a failed heating element. 

Start by cleaning the lint trap and inspecting the exhaust vent for any blockages. Make sure to check the entire length of the vent, including the outside vent hood, as it can get clogged with lint, bird nests, or even a stuck flapper valve. If the vent is clear, you can then test the thermal fuse for continuity with a multimeter. If it's blown, you'll need to replace it before the dryer will heat up again. 

It's also a good idea to check your manual, specifically page 6, for some tips on maintenance and safety to prevent this issue from happening again in the future. Remember to always clean the lint filter and check the vent regularly to ensure your dryer is working efficiently and safely.

retrieved_context:
[page 5] Chapter 5: Dryer Troubleshooting
If the dryer runs but does not heat, the most common causes are a blown thermal fuse, a clogged
exhaust vent restricting airflow, or a failed heating element. These three causes should be checked in
this order, since a restricted vent is the most common root cause and can itself lead to a blown thermal
fuse if left unaddressed.
Start by cleaning the lint trap and inspecting the exhaust vent for lint buildup or blockage along its full
length, including the outside vent hood, which can become blocked by bird nests, lint clumps, or a stuck
flapper valve.
If the vent is clear, the thermal fuse should be tested for continuity with a multimeter; a blown fuse must
be replaced before the dryer will heat again, and usually indicates the vent was restricted at some

[page 6] Chapter 6: Dryer Maintenance and Safety
Have the full exhaust duct professionally cleaned once a year, especially for long duct runs, to reduce
lint accumulation and fire risk; this is the single most effective preventive maintenance step for avoiding
dryer fires, which are most often caused by restricted airflow rather than an electrical fault.
Never operate the dryer without the lint filter in place, and never dry items that have been in contact
with flammable substances such as cooking oil, gasoline, or cleaning solvents, since residual vapors
can ignite from the heating element even after the item appears clean and dry.
If the dryer shuts off mid-cycle repeatedly, this may indicate the thermal fuse is tripping due to restricted

[page 6] airflow rather than a fuse defect alone; inspect the full vent path, including the point where the duct
exits the house, before replacing parts, since replacing the fuse without clearing the vent will simply
cause it to blow again.
Clean the area behind and underneath the dryer periodically, since lint escapes the filter over time and
accumulates in this area, creating an additional fire hazard that is separate from the vent duct itself.
Do not dry rubber-backed rugs, bath mats with rubber soles, or anything containing foam padding, as
the heat can degrade these materials and, in some cases, cause spontaneous combustion during the
cool-down phase after the heat cycle ends.
Keep the area around the dryer clear of storage boxes and clutter, maintaining at least a few inches of

[page 5] be replaced before the dryer will heat again, and usually indicates the vent was restricted at some
point, causing internal temperatures to exceed the fuse's safety threshold.
Error code AF indicates the exhaust vent needs cleaning based on airflow sensor readings, even if the
cycle completes. Error code dF indicates a moisture sensor malfunction. Error code tE indicates a drum
temperature sensor fault, which should be checked by a technician if it recurs after a power cycle.
If the dryer takes multiple cycles to fully dry a load, first rule out an overloaded drum, since the LD-500's
18 lb capacity assumes clothes can tumble freely; a load that does not visibly tumble and fall during the
cycle is likely overloaded regardless of its actual weight.
